Oui, Monsieur nous avons vu un miracle.” CHAPTER 9 1. The SPR is still going, see www.spr.ac.uk . 2. For reviews and meta-analyses of research areas such as mind-matter interaction, mind-organism interaction and dream ESP, see D. Radin, Entangled Minds: Extrasensory Experiences in a Quantum Reality (New York: Pocket Books, 2006). 3. D. W. Moore, “Three in Four Americans Believe in Paranormal: Little Change from Similar Results in 2001,” Gallup , June 16, 2005, www.gallup.com/poll/16915/three-four-americans-believe-paranormal.aspx . 4. Eliade, Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy , trans. W. R Trask (Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, 1972). Originally published in French in 1951. 9. “In the Tsunami’s Wake,” Fortean Times , 196 (2005): 26. 10. “Did Animals Sense Danger?” Fortean Times , 194 (2005): 6. 11. M. Kittenis, P. G. Caryl, and P. Burns (Thorverton, U.K.: Imprint Academic, 2003), 85–109. 20. D. P. Luke, “Paranormal Phenomena and Psychoactive Drugs: Fifty Years of Research,” Bulletin of the Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies 15, no. 3 (2005): 15–16, www.maps.org/news-letters/v15n3/paranormal.pdf . 21. An Investigation into the Ontology of Entheogenic Entity Encounters,” Entheogen Review: The Journal of Unauthorized Research on Visionary Plants and Drugs 17, no. 1 (2008): 1–9 and 38–40. CHAPTER 15 1. Stuart W. Twemlow, Glen O. Gabbard, and Fowler C. Jones, “The Out-of-Body Experience: A Phenomenological Typology Based on Questionnaire Responses,” American Journal of Psychiatry 139, no 4 (1982): 450–55. 2. Robert Waggoner, Lucid Dreaming: Gateway to the Inner Self (Needham, MA: Moment Point Press, 2009). 3. Thomas Campbell, My Big Toe (n.p.: Lightening Strike Books, 2003). 4. Thomas Yuschek, Advanced Lucid Dreaming (n.p.: Lulu, 2006), 9. 5. Thomas Yuschek, Advanced Lucid Dreaming (n.p.: Lulu, 2006), 164. 6. Robert Monroe, Journeys Out of the Body (New York: Broadway, 2001), 26. 7. Stuart W. Twemlow and Glen O. Gabbard, With Eyes of the Mind: An Empirical Analysis of Out-of-the-Body States (New York: Praeger, 1984). 8. Ian Wilson, interview by Robert Waggoner, The Lucid Dream Exchange , June 2010, www.dreaminglucid.com/dreamspeak.html . CHAPTER 16 1. J. A. Cheyne, S. D. Rueffer, and I. R. Newby-Clarke, “Hypnagogic and Hypnopompic Hallucinations during Sleep Paralysis: Neurological and Cultural Construction of the Nightmare,” Consciousness and Cognition 8 (1999): 319–37. 2. P. Marquet, J. Peters, J. Aerts, G. Delfiore, C. Deguelfre, A. Luxen, and G. Franck, “Functional Neuroanatomy of Human Rapid-eye-movement Sleep and Dreaming,” Nature 383 (1996): 163–66. 3. M. Winkelman, “Spirits as Human Nature and the Fundamental Structures of Consciousness,” in Shaman to Scientist , ed. J. Houran (Lanham, MD: Scarecrow Press, 2004), 59–96. 4. J. A. Hobson, Dreaming: An Introduction to the Science of Sleep (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2002). Hobson and other dream researchers still debate if REM = dreaming, but we can safely say that most dreams we remember come from this physiological state. 5. The best introduction to Jung is his autobiography, Memories, Dreams, and Reflections . James Hillman’s book The Soul’s Code (New York: Warner Books, 1996), while not explicitly about dreams, showcases his theory of the personality and its inner workings. 6. R. Bly, A Little Book on the Human Shadow (New York: HarperOne, 1988). 7. For more about myth, landscape, and unconscious acting out, see C. Chalquist, Terrapsychology: Reengaging the Soul of Place (New Orleans: Spring Journal Books, 2007). 8. S. Sherwood, “Relationship between the Hypnagogic/Hypnopompic States and Reports of Anomalous Experiences,” Journal of Parapsychology 66 (2002): 127–50. Stan Krippner and Montague Ullman’s work at the Dream Laboratory of the Maimonides Medical Center in the 1970s has proven to be the zenith of scientific work on psychic dreams; but the evidence is well known to lifelong dreamers. 9. H.
